name: Chromatic
what_it_does: Visual regression testing service for Storybook that captures UI component snapshots, detects pixel-level changes across builds, and integrates into PR review workflows for human or automated approval.
use_cases: Run the Chromatic CLI in CI to publish Storybook stories and automatically detect visual regressions in UI components introduced by a code change
Integrate Chromatic's REST API into an agent-driven CI pipeline to check build status and retrieve per-story diff results without human intervention
Use TurboSnap to limit snapshot captures to only stories affected by changed files, reducing CI cost and time for large component libraries
Automate baseline acceptance in non-PR workflows (e.g., nightly builds) using the --auto-accept-changes flag so agents can gate deployments on visual pass/fail
Retrieve build and snapshot results via the Chromatic API to feed visual regression data into an agent that generates change summaries or files issues
not_for: End-to-end UI testing with user interactions — Chromatic tests static Storybook story renders only, not full page flows; use Playwright or Cypress for E2E
Projects not using Storybook — Chromatic is tightly coupled to Storybook as the story source and has no support for other component testing frameworks
Fully offline or air-gapped CI environments — Chromatic requires uploading stories to the Chromatic cloud service for rendering and diffing
best_when: Your project uses Storybook and you want automated visual regression gating in CI pull request workflows with minimal setup and a managed snapshot infrastructure.
avoid_when: You need on-premise visual testing with no data leaving your network, or your UI testing strategy is interaction-based rather than component-snapshot-based.

auth.notes: Authentication via a project token passed as --project-token to the Chromatic CLI or as CHROMATIC_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable. The API also uses project tokens for programmatic access.

pricing.paid_tiers: Paid plans from ~$149/month for more snapshots and team features; enterprise pricing available
pricing.notes: Free tier covers individual developers and small projects. Snapshot overage charges apply on paid plans. TurboSnap can significantly reduce snapshot consumption on free tier.

performance.rate_limits: Snapshot limits per billing plan; no documented API rate limits for build submission

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: CLI exit codes reliably indicate pass/fail for CI integration. Build status and per-story diff counts are available via the GraphQL API for programmatic agent consumption.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Re-running a Chromatic build for the same commit creates a new build rather than being idempotent; baseline management means re-runs may produce different acceptance states.
known_agent_gotchas: The Chromatic CLI exit code 1 is used for both build failures (infrastructure error) and visual change detection (expected behavior) — agents must parse CLI output or use the API to distinguish between a broken build and a pending review

Baseline snapshots are anchored to the git branch history; an agent running builds on ephemeral branches or detached HEAD states may fail to find a baseline and capture all stories as new, inflating snapshot counts
TurboSnap requires a clean git working tree and accurate --storybook-config-dir; misconfigured TurboSnap silently falls back to full capture, consuming full snapshot quota without warning
The GraphQL API requires knowing the build ID returned by the CLI; there is no endpoint to list recent builds by commit SHA without first querying the project's build history

Snapshot counts apply per story per browser per viewport — a single Storybook story tested in 2 browsers at 3 viewports consumes 6 snapshots; agents triggering frequent builds can exhaust free tier quotas quickly

security_score_components.security_notes: Project tokens are long-lived and scoped per project; there is no token expiry or rotation mechanism. Store CHROMATIC_PROJECT_TOKEN as a CI secret, never in source code. UI component snapshots are uploaded to Chromatic's cloud infrastructure.
